turns.user_message: Honestly, I'm feeling a bit frustrated. I've been trying to quit for years, but I always end up relapsing. My grandmother was just diagnosed with lung cancer, and that's what triggered my latest attempt to quit. I've been trying for a few weeks now, but it's tough. I smoke 10-15 cigarettes a day, and it's hard to imagine my life without them.
step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step2_rag_chunks: Talking to someone about your cravings can help reduce the intensity of the urge.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.

A craving is not a command â€” it is a wave. It builds, peaks, and fades, usually within 3 to 5 minutes. Urge surfing means observing the craving without acting on it â€” noticing where you feel it in your body, watching it get stronger, and then watching it pass. Each time you surf an urge without smoking, it gets slightly easier the next time.

step2_rag_chunks: When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.
Exercise can help improve mood and reduce nicotine cravings.
